Dr. Eliane, thank you for the counteroffer. We can move a little on the price, but the two-year term is non-negotiable.
Thank you, Victor. You have already moved on the price, so it can't be your bottom line. Let me offer one concession: I will accept two years if you shorten the delivery window.
That is fair. The window is one clause where we might have some leeway.
Good. Then let us meet halfway: six weeks instead of ten. If you can do that, I think we have a deal.
Let me check with my team. I do not want us to reach a deadlock over two weeks.
Neither do I. We are negotiating in good faith, and I would rather close than walk away over a small point.
They said yes. Six weeks it is. I will update that clause and send the revised contract tonight.
Perfect. I will read every line, and once it is fully binding, I will sign off and we start.
A pleasure doing business with you, Doctor. You must have negotiated before, you read the room well.
You have moved on the price, so it can't be your bottom line.
Let me offer one concession to move this forward.
Let us meet halfway so we do not reach a deadlock.
This clause is non-negotiable for us; it protects our patients.
I will sign off once the contract is fully binding.

Hi Eliane, it is your negotiation coach. Before your call tomorrow, let me give you one idea: read the other side. They have already sent a counteroffer, so they must want to close before the quarter ends. If the price were really non-negotiable, they would not have moved at all, so it can't be their true bottom line. When they say a clause is fixed, they might be testing you, or they might simply have little leeway. Do not walk away too early. Offer a small concession, and watch how they react. If they meet you halfway, you know a deal is close. If they reach a deadlock and stop moving, they must have hit a real limit. Stay calm, keep it in good faith, and do not sign off until every term is binding and clear.

Hi team, it is Eliane. Let me recap where we are with the supplier so we can plan. They sent a counteroffer this morning, which tells me they must be keen to close. Their price dropped a little, so the first number can't have been their real bottom line. They said one clause is non-negotiable, but I think they might have some leeway there, because they moved on everything else. We are not at a deadlock yet. My plan is simple: I will offer one concession on the delivery window, and if they meet us halfway, we sign. If they walk away, we know the terms were never going to work. Either way, I will not sign off until the contract is fully binding. I want to keep this in good faith, but I also want to protect our margin.
